taskDescription: Click on the key anatomical terms related to hip joint anatomy in the text below.
textField:
The hip joint is a *synovial* *ball-and-socket* joint formed between the *acetabulum* of the hip bone and the head of the *femur*. The *acetabular* *labrum* deepens the socket and enhances stability. The joint capsule is reinforced by three ligaments: the *iliofemoral*, *pubofemoral*, and *ischiofemoral* ligaments.

The blood supply to the femoral head is primarily from the *medial* *circumflex* femoral artery via *retinacular* vessels that ascend along the femoral neck. The *ligamentum* *teres* carries a small artery (branch of the *obturator* artery) that is important in children but insufficient in adults. *Avascular* *necrosis* is a dreaded complication of femoral neck fractures due to disruption of the retinacular vessels.

The main muscles acting on the hip include the *iliopsoas* (chief flexor), *gluteus* *maximus* (chief extensor), and *gluteus* *medius* (chief abductor). A positive *Trendelenburg* sign indicates weakness of the hip abductors, typically gluteus medius.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Some key terms were missed.
Excellent! You identified the key hip joint terms.
